etymology.description: Depicts a human with crossed legs -> today's form shows upper part similar to 立(立) (lì, 'stand')
etymology.interpretation: Originally depicted a human figure with crossed legs, visually suggesting the notion of crossing or mixing, as legs are intertwined. This cross-legged posture naturally extended to meanings associated with mixing or crossing paths.

examples.chinese: 我们必须把材料交在一起(我們必須把材料交在一起)。
examples.pinyin: Wǒmen bìxū bǎ cáiliào xiāo zài yīqǐ.
examples.english: We must mix the materials together.

examples.chinese: 在烹饪课上(在烹飪課上)，老师教我们如何交不同的配料(老師教我們如何交不同的配料)。
examples.pinyin: Zài pēngrèn kè shàng, lǎoshī jiāo wǒmen rúhé xiāo bùtóng de pèiliào.
examples.english: In the cooking class, the teacher taught us how to mix different ingredients.

examples.chinese: 把这两种颜料交在一起(把這兩種顏料交在一起)，可以得到一种新的颜色(可以得到一種新的顏色)。
examples.pinyin: Bǎ zhè liǎng zhǒng yánliào xiāo zài yīqǐ, kěyǐ dédào yī zhǒng xīn de yánsè.
examples.english: By mixing these two paints together, you can get a new color.
